sources.note: Pike County IN rural data seed v1 — 2025 snapshot for ReClaw dev & testing
Simulated public records from county auditor / GIS style exports

property_records.notes: Mostly tillable, small barn, no well currently active
Town lot, needs roof work. Good candidate for 'fixer rural starter'
Large parcel, two outbuildings, creek access. Rare for under $150k.
Very low entry price. High risk of foundation issues per local chatter.

budgets.notes: Slight deficit. Road maintenance and bridge projects are the big pressure items. Property tax levy up ~4.2% YoY.
Small town, surprisingly balanced. Heavy reliance on utility transfers.

research.summary: Pike County (pop ~12,200) shows classic rural Indiana patterns: low property values, ag-heavy tax base, and tightening budgets. Winslow (pop ~780) has extremely affordable entry points — multiple habitable parcels under $30k. County is running a modest deficit in 2025 driven by road/bridge needs. Public payroll is lean; deputy and road crew pay hovers in the low-to-mid $40ks. Several large ag parcels remain surprisingly cheap by national standards, creating both opportunity and 'why is it still available' questions.

insights.title: Pike County spends heavily on roads — classic rural pressure point
insights.detail: Road & bridge category is one of the largest line items. In low-tax-base counties this is often the first thing to show visible decay.
insights.suggested_angle: Show before/after photos of county roads + explain where the money actually goes.

insights.title: 2 parcels under $35k assessed in Winslow area
insights.detail: Extremely low entry prices. Good for 'can you actually live on this' stories and land-banking angles. Also flags potential maintenance nightmares or title issues.
insights.suggested_angle: The $25k house in rural Indiana — what's the real monthly cost?

insights.title: Large acreage still trading under $150k — watch for consolidation or outside money
insights.detail: 47-acre example at ~$124k assessed is cheap by almost any national metric. Either the land has serious limitations or the local market hasn't caught up to remote buyer interest.
insights.suggested_angle: Why 40+ acres in southern Indiana is still under $130k (and whether you want it)

insights.title: Public sector pay remains very low vs national rural averages
insights.detail: Deputy and road crew roles in the low $40ks. This is both a 'people can't afford to live here on these wages' story and a 'why recruitment is impossible' story.
insights.suggested_angle: What a $47k sheriff deputy job actually buys you in Pike County 2026

red_flags.description: Pike County FY2025 running $148,000 deficit (2.9% of spend).
red_flags.recommended_action: Watch next levy hearing and road project bids. Content angle: 'Why your rural county is quietly going broke on bridges'.

red_flags.description: Median assessed value in sample is only $38,500. Signals either very old housing stock, lack of economic activity, or both.
red_flags.recommended_action: Cross-reference with building permits and demolition orders. Good 'dying small town' or 'quiet revival' bifurcation story.

red_flags.description: Deficit + heavy road spend = classic rural trap. They are already underwater and the biggest expense category is non-optional.
red_flags.recommended_action: Track the next cumulative bridge fund levy attempt. This is often where small counties quietly add 10-15% to tax bills.

budget_implications: Pike County will likely raise property taxes or cut maintenance in 2026 to close the gap.
content_angles: Top 2 cheapest habitable properties in Pike County right now (and what they actually need)
analysis.summary: Pike / Winslow shows the typical rural squeeze: low asset values, structural budget pressure on infrastructure, and wages that make it hard for working families to stay. 3 red flags and 4 insights extracted. Strong material for both cautionary and 'last cheap land' style faceless content. | GATE WARNING: QUALITY GATE FAILURES: Risk score 8.4 is very high. Manual review recommended before Obsidian publish.
